QF.id: IV Introduction to Interest Rate Derivatives_18

answer.text: Buying a cap and buying a floor simultaneously
Buying a cap and selling a floor simultaneously
Selling both a cap and a floor
Buying two caps with different strikes

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** An interest rate collar = simultaneous purchase of an interest rate cap + sale of an interest rate floor on the same underlying, maturity, and notional. The premium received from selling the floor partially offsets the cost of buying the cap.
**The Trap:** A reverse collar is the opposite — buying a floor and selling a cap. A collar is a cost-reduction strategy where you give up some upside (the floor limits your benefit from falling rates) to reduce the cost of protection (cap premium).
**Cross-Exam Context:** Collars are tested in Series IV as OTC option strategies. Similar structures exist in equity and currency derivative exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Zero-cost collars are popular — strike rates are chosen so that cap premium = floor premium. This gives free protection against rate rises but caps the benefit from rate falls.
